sections.summary: Scope applies to fruits dried by natural or artificial means (whole or subdivided as appropriate) including apples, apricots, peaches, pears, nectarines, prunes, figs, dates, and vine fruits (raisins, currants), permitting preparatory operations prior to drying and excluding 'dehydrated fruits' with moisture content not exceeding 5%.
States that no definitions are considered necessary for this code of practice.

Sets raw material requirements for dried fruits including sanitary disposal of human/animal wastes, potable irrigation water, pest and disease control conducted per official recommendations and supervised personnel, cleanable containers and equipment, sanitary harvesting/production techniques, segregation and disposal of unfit material, protection from contamination, and storage/processing timing and fumigation where appropriate.

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR DRIED FRUITS (CXC 3-1969)
sections.summary: Code title with detailed hygienic guidance for dried-fruit operations covering drying-yard siting and construction, cutting-shed design and protections, storage protection from pests and contamination, requirement for potable water (per WHO 1971) for washing/cleaning, hygienic upkeep of trays/equipment, approved fumigation, and transport facilities and handling procedures.

Specifies sanitary facilities and controls: physical separation of raw and finished-product areas, potable water and ice per WHO standards, dedicated lines for non-potable water, plumbing and waste disposal sized and approved by the official agency, adequate lighting and ventilation, and provision/maintenance of toilet and hand-washing facilities with hygiene notices.

Requires equipment and utensils to use appropriate food-contact materials (smooth, non-toxic, cleanable), sanitary design and installation permitting thorough cleaning, identification/separation of equipment for inedible uses, and drying equipment constructed and operated so the product is not adversely affected.

Mandates that all food-contact surfaces be smooth, free of pits/crevices/loose scale, non-toxic, unaffected by food, and capable of withstanding repeated cleaning, with non-absorbent materials preferred unless a specific acceptable process requires otherwise (e.g., wood).

Requires equipment and utensils to be designed and constructed to prevent hygienic hazards and to permit easy and thorough cleaning, and stationary equipment to be installed to allow easy, thorough cleaning.
Mandates that equipment and utensils used for inedible or contaminating materials be clearly identified and not used for handling edible products.
Requires drying equipment to be constructed and operated so that the drying medium cannot adversely affect the product.

Sets minimal hygienic operating requirements for all food production, handling, storage and distribution, covering sanitary maintenance, vermin control, exclusion of domestic animals, personnel health, and controls on toxic substances, each with specified obligations.

sections.section: 1 Sanitary maintenance of plant, facilities and premises
sections.summary: Requires buildings, equipment, utensils and other plant facilities to be kept in good repair, clean and orderly with frequent removal of waste, adequate receptacles, and use of appropriate detergents/disinfectants that do not present public health hazards.
Requires effective measures to prevent entrance and harbourage of insects, rodents, birds or other vermin on the premises.
Requires dogs, cats and other domestic animals to be excluded from areas where food is processed or stored.

Obliges management to require personnel to report infected wounds or illnesses and to prevent anyone known to have a communicable disease, be a carrier, or have infected wounds from working where they could contaminate food or food-contact surfaces.

Requires rodenticides, fumigants, insecticides and other toxic substances to be stored in separate locked rooms or cabinets and handled or used only by, or under direct supervision of, properly trained personnel aware of contamination hazards.

sections.section: 6) Personnel hygiene and food handling practices
sections.summary: Requires personnel to maintain high personal cleanliness and appropriate clean clothing/headdress, wash hands as needed, prohibit spitting/eating/tobacco in food areas, take precautions to prevent product contamination, treat and cover minor hand injuries with waterproof dressings and provide first‑aid facilities, and keep gloves clean, sanitary and impermeable except where inappropriate.

sections.section: D. Operating Practices and Production Requirements
sections.summary: Sets operating and production requirements covering raw material acceptance, storage and water quality; inspection, sorting and washing of raw materials; timing and sanitary conduct of preparatory and packaging operations; preservation methods (including approved chemical preservatives, drying, heat processing and hermetic sealing) to prevent infestation, contamination or spoilage; and storage/transport conditions to prevent microbial or pest-related deterioration, with specific guidance for dried fruit fumigation or cold storage.

Stipulates that raw materials known to contain decomposed, toxic or extraneous substances not removable by normal sorting/preparation should not be accepted, and that stored raw materials must be protected against contamination, infestation and deterioration.

Requires water used to convey raw materials to be from a safe source or suitably treated and used with official permission, and mandates inspection, sorting or culling of raw materials in a clean sanitary manner so only clean, sound materials are processed.

Recommends that each plant have access to laboratory control of sanitary quality—varying by product and need—with rejection of fruits unfit for consumption and use of recognized standard analytical methods.

Specifies end-product requirements and testing: use appropriate sampling/analysis methods, products should be free from objectionable matter, contain no pathogenic microorganisms or microbial toxins, and comply with Codex limits on pesticide residues and food additives.
